problem_summary: 2007 MCM/ICM Problem C (interdisciplinary): design and evaluate a better organ-allocation/transplant network for the U.S., judging "effectiveness" against medical, geographic, ethical, and political constraints. Team 2052 narrows the open-ended problem to KIDNEY allocation (kidneys = 58.9% of all transplants, and uniquely support live/paired donation) and attacks it on three fronts: (1) a discrete-event simulation of the national network to compare structural policies, (2) kidney-exchange optimization (list-paired donation) to grow effective supply, and (3) a formal analytic decision model for an individual patient choosing whether to accept an organ now or wait. They close with explicit ethics/policy recommendations (presumed consent, expense-reimbursement compensation) delivered as letters to Congress and the HRSA director.
models_used: Discrete-event Monte Carlo simulation of the transplant network, advanced one day per time-step over many averaged runs (implemented in C++ with patient/organ objects carrying blood type, HLA, PRA, age, race, disease)
Network-as-rooted-tree model: root = national network, children = regions, leaves = Organ Procurement Centers; each node's wait list is the concatenation of its children's lists
Poisson arrival processes for both new patients and new cadaver organs, with rate gamma estimated as (annual new applicants)/365.25
'Bottom-up priority rules': recursive allocation traversing leaves->root, encoding OPTN's local-then-regional-then-national matching and HLA/blood-type/PRA priority tiers

Time-dependent mortality (survival) model: per-patient death rate treated as a linear function k = aT + b of wait time T, giving survival e^{-(aT+b)T}; coefficients a,b regressed from OPTN 1995-2004 active-waitlist data

Linear regression to calibrate model parameters: death-rate coefficients, the doctor-experience-vs-success relationship, and the cold-ischemia/distance penalty (~5% success drop for cross-center transport)

Maximal-matching graph algorithm for kidney paired donation (reviewed from literature) plus an implemented list-paired-donation (LPD) exchange mechanism using expected donor blood types inferred from a patient's phenotype (each patient contributes ~r expected donors to the pool)

Analytic optimal-stopping / rational-choice decision model for the individual patient: separable survival functions s_i(t0,t)=a_i(t0)b_i(t-t0), Poisson-availability of future alternatives (prob e^{-p_i t} of not yet arriving), expected-lifespan maximization

Theorem + proof (via double-integral separation and integration by parts) establishing a threshold/monotone optimal policy, yielding a concrete accept/wait heuristic that orders alternatives by integral-of-b and walks down from the best

Multi-objective evaluation: six explicit objective functions (raw successful transplants; age-weighted variants; healthy-vs-sick weighting with parameter alpha; max-wait-time penalty) used to score policies
key_techniques: Aggressive but well-justified scoping: reduce the whole organ system to kidneys because kidneys dominate volume AND uniquely enable live/paired donation, making the most interesting modeling live there
Separation of concerns into three complementary models at different scales: system-level simulation, supply-side exchange optimization, and individual-level analytic decision theory

Encoding real-world policy (OPTN rules, Eurotransplant point system) directly as tunable simulation parameters, so policy comparison becomes a controlled experiment (single national queue vs 20 regional queues; OPTN vs Eurotransplant points; with/without LPD)

Driving ethics through the objective function rather than hacking the mechanism: 'save the kids'/penalize-the-elderly/terminal-illness preferences are implemented purely by changing the returns the simulation maximizes, keeping the core model untouched

Defining a clean mathematical abstraction (separable survival functions) precisely to exclude pathological cases and make the optimal-stopping problem tractable, then honestly stress-testing where separability breaks
Calibration against real OPTN national data (1995-2004) by feeding in actual yearly donation counts and regressing model coefficients
Parameter sweeps: graphing queue size and every objective function against donation rate (0.2-2.0) and against the expected-donors-per-patient parameter r
Translating quantitative findings into actionable, audience-specific deliverables (letter to Congress on network structure; letter to HRSA director on consent/compensation/experience effects)
why_it_won: "It treated an open-ended interdisciplinary problem the way a good researcher would: it made one defensible scoping decision (kidneys) and then built three genuinely different kinds of model that reinforce each other, rather than one monolithic model. The simulation is grounded in the actual OPTN rule structure and calibrated to real national data, so its policy comparisons (regional vs national queue, OPTN vs Eurotransplant, list-paired exchange) read as credible controlled experiments, not hand-waving. The standout is the patient-decision section: a clean formal abstraction (separable survival functions), a stated theorem, and a real proof yielding an implementable accept-or-wait heuristic. Judges reward an 'aha' theorem amid a simulation paper, and this team delivered one. Crucially, it engages the ethics/politics dimension that Problem C demands head-on and quantitatively, by folding moral preferences into the objective function and then issuing concrete, well-reasoned policy recommendations (keep regional allocation, adopt presumed consent, pilot expense-reimbursement compensation) in the form of letters to actual decision-makers. The team is also unusually self-aware: it explicitly names the realism-vs-elegance tradeoff and the objective-function-choice problem as weaknesses, which signals modeling maturity."
section_highlights: "Introduction does heavy lifting: it teaches the medical background (ABO blood type, HLA antigens, PRA sensitization, live vs cadaveric kidney half-life, KPD vs LPD) so every later modeling choice is legible, and it ends with an explicit roadmap of the three-part attack. The generic-network section states the rooted-tree abstraction and the four-phase daily time-step (add patients, add organs, allocate bottom-up, simulate deaths) cleanly, with the Poisson and linear-death-rate assumptions boxed in an explicit Summary of Assumptions. A labeled flowchart visualizes the simulation loop. The patient-choice section is the intellectual centerpiece: it builds formal Definitions 7.1-7.2 (separable survival functions, ordering by integral of b), states Theorem 7.3, gives a full integration-by-parts proof, and distills it into a numbered 6-step heuristic a real patient/doctor could follow. The paper uses parameter-sweep line charts (objective functions and queue size vs donation rate; results vs experience effect) to make simulation output legible. Two appendix letters (to Congress, to the HRSA director) translate results into plain-language, audience-targeted policy advice. The Strengths/Weaknesses/Future Considerations section is candid and specific (flexibility of the C++ object model; over-realism vs elegance; fragility of embedded numbers; the unresolved question of which objective is 'correct')."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-acknowledged 'over-realism': the simulation produces few clean, quotable results and lacks the 'aha' clarity of a closed-form result; much of the policy comparison reduces to 'little difference' (e.g., OPTN vs Eurotransplant)

Embedded numbers are not airtight - HLA-matching importance lacks literature consensus, and OPTN-derived parameters can't guarantee numerical accuracy; conclusions rest on a calibration the authors themselves flag as soft

The entire simulation verdict hinges on the choice of objective function, and the team admits it 'barely explores the vast space' and may miss the correct one entirely - a deep validity threat for an ethics-laden problem

Several modeling assumptions are strong conveniences: constant Poisson availability probability p_i per future alternative, separability of survival functions (admitted to fail at extreme old age), patient can receive only one transplant, and patients purely maximize expected lifespan (no quality-of-life or cost)

The math has visible transcription/typo issues in this version (e.g., survival written as e^{2aT+a+b} where an exponent sign is garbled; a Poisson pmf rendered with e^{gamma} rather than e^{-gamma}; truncated integral bounds), suggesting rushed final typesetting

The list-paired-donation supply gain depends on an unvalidated assumption that a 'random percentage of a predefined donor base' actually donates and on inferring expected donor blood types from phenotype - the team concedes the empirical basis is inconclusive

Charts appear illustrative/idealized (suspiciously smooth, evenly-spaced curves), and the doctor-experience effect that would favor centralization is shown not to exist in the data, so one of the two structural levers is largely hypothetical
reusable_patterns: Scope a sprawling open problem to the highest-leverage sub-case and justify the choice with data (volume + unique modeling richness), then explicitly note the framework generalizes

Build a portfolio of models at different scales (macro simulation + supply-side combinatorial optimization + micro analytic decision model) so they triangulate the same problem instead of betting everything on one approach

Model an institutional network as a rooted tree with leaf-level arrivals and recursive bottom-up allocation - a clean, reusable abstraction for any regionalized/hierarchical distribution system (logistics, supply chains, emergency services)

Use Poisson processes for independent arrivals and a time-dependent (linear-in-wait-time) hazard/death rate; calibrate the hazard coefficients by regression against real historical counts fed through the simulation
Encode competing real-world policies as swappable parameter sets so policy comparison becomes a controlled simulation experiment with everything else held fixed

Inject ethical/value judgments through the objective function, not the mechanism - define several objective functions, name your favored one, and report results under each to expose the value-sensitivity of conclusions

When you need an analytic result, first define a clean structural restriction (here, separable survival functions) that excludes pathological cases, prove a monotone/threshold optimality theorem, then convert it into a numbered step-by-step heuristic an end user can run
Do parameter sweeps over the key uncertain drivers and plot every objective against them to show robustness and locate regime changes
Close with audience-specific deliverables (letters/memos to the actual decision-makers named in the prompt) that translate quantitative findings into concrete recommendations
Write a candid Strengths/Weaknesses section that names the core tradeoffs (realism vs elegance, objective-function choice) - demonstrating modeling maturity reads as a strength to judges
